cue: How do you tell someone to get lost?

es.literal: to send to the fifth pine tree
es.meaning: To send someone packing; to tell them to get lost.
es.example: Se puso pesado y lo mandé al quinto pino.
es.exampleTranslation: He got annoying so I told him to get lost.
es.regionNote: Strongly Peninsular. Mexico prefers 'mandar a volar' or 'mandar por un tubo'. Reviewer should pick the MX-default form; this may re-pair.

it.meaning: To tell someone to get lost.
it.example: Si è messo a insistere e l'ho mandato a quel paese.
it.exampleTranslation: He kept pushing so I told him where to go.

bridge.note: Both languages banish the nuisance to a vague far-off place, and decline to name it. Spanish sends them to the FIFTH PINE — a specific tree in no particular forest, the fifth of nothing. Italian sends them to THAT VILLAGE — everyone knows which, nobody will say. A precise absurdity versus a knowing vagueness, and both are polite stand-ins for something much ruder.

interference.es_to_it: Pines mean nothing here; Italian names a coy destination: a quel paese. Both are euphemisms for a cruder send-off.
interference.it_to_es: 'Mandar a ese pueblo' is not the Spanish idiom, and the Italian is Peninsular-flavoured anyway — in Mexico, mandar a volar.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Both are the polite face of something vulgar — a register entry about euphemism. ES side is very Peninsular; flagged for MX re-pairing.
